catgraph 0.10.0
  • Reference
    • Comparing catgraph modality networks with MCA and bipartite approaches
    • Introduction to catgraph
  • Changelog

Build a modality-level association graph

Source: R/build_modality_graph.R
build_modality_graph.Rd

Constructs a modality-level association network in which each node is a category level, encoded as "variable=level", and each edge represents the pairwise association between two modalities from different variables.

Usage

build_modality_graph(data, remove_na = TRUE, min_count = 1L)

Arguments

data

A data frame of categorical variables.

remove_na

Logical. If TRUE, rows with missing values are removed before graph construction. Default is TRUE.

min_count

Integer. Modalities with total count strictly below this threshold are removed before association computation. Default is 1.

Value

An object of class "catmodgraph", containing:

graph

An igraph object.

modalities

A data frame describing node identities.

indicator_matrix

The binary modality indicator matrix.

data

The processed categorical data used to build the graph.

Details

Edge weights (weight) are absolute phi coefficients computed from the corresponding binary 2x2 indicator table, so that edge thickness in plots always scales with association strength regardless of direction. The signed phi coefficient is stored separately as phi_signed. The signed standardized residual is stored as std_resid: positive values indicate co-occurrence above expectation (attraction), whereas negative values indicate co-occurrence below expectation (repulsion). The graph is therefore weighted by association magnitude and annotated by direction.

Same-variable modality pairs are excluded by construction, because such modalities are mutually exclusive and are not meaningful as co-occurrence edges in the intended network.

Interpretation. This graph is descriptive and exploratory. It is not a causal graph, not a respondent-segmentation model, and not a conditional-independence graph. Because edge weights are absolute values, users should inspect std_resid or use signed plotting options when distinguishing attraction from repulsion matters.

Implementation. Since v0.8.0 the per-pair 2x2 statistics are computed in vectorised closed form from the modality co-occurrence matrix \(M^\top M\), rather than by an inner loop over chisq.test calls. For \(m\) modalities on \(n\) rows the dominant cost drops from \(O(m^2 n)\) plus a per-pair chisq.test overhead to a single \(O(m^2 n)\) matrix product plus \(O(m^2)\) arithmetic. Typical speed-up on realistic data (\(m \approx 40\), \(n \approx 5000\)) is 30–100x. Numeric output is algebraically identical to the earlier loop implementation up to floating-point tolerance; edge ordering in the returned graph may differ.

This function is the modality-level counterpart to catgraph().

Examples

df <- expand_table(Titanic)
mg <- build_modality_graph(df)
mg
#> $graph
#> IGRAPH 224f809 UNW- 10 36 -- 
#> + attr: name (v/c), variable (v/c), modality (v/c), weight (e/n),
#> | phi_signed (e/n), p_value (e/n), std_resid (e/n)
#> + edges from 224f809 (vertex names):
#>  [1] Class=1st --Sex=Female  Class=2nd --Sex=Female  Class=3rd --Sex=Female 
#>  [4] Class=Crew--Sex=Female  Class=1st --Sex=Male    Class=2nd --Sex=Male   
#>  [7] Class=3rd --Sex=Male    Class=Crew--Sex=Male    Class=1st --Age=Adult  
#> [10] Class=2nd --Age=Adult   Class=3rd --Age=Adult   Class=Crew--Age=Adult  
#> [13] Sex=Female--Age=Adult   Sex=Male  --Age=Adult   Class=1st --Age=Child  
#> [16] Class=2nd --Age=Child   Class=3rd --Age=Child   Class=Crew--Age=Child  
#> [19] Sex=Female--Age=Child   Sex=Male  --Age=Child   Class=1st --Survived=No
#> + ... omitted several edges
#> 
#> $modalities
#>            node variable modality
#> 1     Class=1st    Class      1st
#> 2     Class=2nd    Class      2nd
#> 3     Class=3rd    Class      3rd
#> 4    Class=Crew    Class     Crew
#> 5    Sex=Female      Sex   Female
#> 6      Sex=Male      Sex     Male
#> 7     Age=Adult      Age    Adult
#> 8     Age=Child      Age    Child
#> 9   Survived=No Survived       No
#> 10 Survived=Yes Survived      Yes
#> 
#> $indicator_matrix
#>         Class=1st Class=2nd Class=3rd Class=Crew Sex=Female Sex=Male Age=Adult
#>    [1,]         0         0         1          0          0        1         0
